speaker: interviewer
text: '94 In Beverly Hills, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was giving me an awful lot of trouble. And he said, you Jews, you just want to have an easy life. You want to go run away from all responsibility, you know what I mean? And he was terrible, anti-Semitic remarks, you know what I mean? You remember, that was 1948. But then the Communists started starting and that's when the anti-Semitism started, when the Communists came in power. And I didn't really know what to say. And all of a sudden, he hears a call that has to go to the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another guy comes over and he says, oh, you don't-- he say, how lucky can you be? My god, why don't you put me in the suitcase, take me with me And I told him, that guy gave me a lot of trouble. He says, don't worry about it. He put us aside, you know what I mean? And once we crossed that line, we were already over the inspection. And that's how we came to USA. And what happened to you once you got to the United States? What was your life? What's your life, like, been since?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- when we came to United States, we flew to New York. In New York, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had $20, $10 each of us. I had $10. My wife had $10. When we passed immigration, they took us $4 each, hat tax. So we had left-- what? $12.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I took a [INAUDIBLE] to LaGuardia, and I had to-- I knew I had to get to Grand Central Station. So I took a cab there to Grand Central Station. When I arrived, I didn't know if I had enough money or not, what can he do to me? He told me it's $11. So I paid him the $11, OK? So I had $1 left.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Albany, New York, where Rose Jackson-- you know, Rose Jackson-- was there already-- my brother's wife. She came earlier. And we stayed there about a week and then came to Los Angeles. I had an aunt here. When I came to Los Angeles, I was here one day. Next, I started working as a busboy at a delicatessen on Pico and Robertson [INAUDIBLE] Then at night, I took a job again. And I was working four hours for [INAUDIBLE] Cafeteria, also busboy.

speaker: interviewer
text: After I'd been there about a month, at [INAUDIBLE], he told me, you're too good for busboy. Come behind the counter. Because I had eventually-- I did speak English, you know what I mean? It wasn't perfect, it wasn't [INAUDIBLE], but it was enough to get by. And I worked there. I know I was at $0.75 an hour. And then I told him I would like to have $1, you know? He says, no way. We don't raise here. This is America. OK. My day off was Wednesday, always after six days. I went to the Jewish Employment Office, it was on Vermont, and I told him I'm looking for a job. He says, well what are you doing? I'm a delicatessen man. Oh, we have opening in Boyle Heights. You know, you know, at that time it was a very strong Jewish neighborhood. I went there Wednesday morning and I told them-- the Jewish-- was name-- the name of the owner was Mr. Berger He told me, I'm so sorry. I already hired somebody. Look, he's here. But you know what? If you want to work for nothing today, I'll give you lunch, he said. I want to see what you are. Maybe it doesn't work out with the other guy. So I worked there. When the day was over, he went to the other guy, whispered something to him, and he said, you're hired. He hired me as a deli man. When he came to me, well, where did you work? I told him I worked four years in New York. I wouldn't dare-- I was lying to him, you know? He says, oh? So show me what you know. I told him, no, Mr. Berger I'll tell you what I want you to do. I want you to show me how you want me to do it for you, because I want to do it the way you want it, not the way they taught me in New York. He says, I like that quality, he says. Go ahead, I'll show you how to take the slicer apart. I told him-- I'd never seen a slicer or taken it apart, you know what I mean? And I now have those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked there for one year, when he sold the store to a family which did not need help. From there I went to work in a deli on Fairfax, on Oakwood and Fairfax in the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Naturally, when I came there and he-- Mr. Berger, can you imagine? I asked for $1. Berger paid me already $1.75 without any hesitation. And when I came on Fairfax, he paid me $2.50, a year later. Which was OK, you know what I mean, for the eight hours a day. No slave labor, not work an hour afterwards. Not a-- no, no, just eight hours. 15 minute lunch. You want to eat a sandwich, eat. How much I owe you? Forget it. You know what I mean? Very menschy. You know what I mean? And I worked there. And then the partners-- happened, something between the two partners. There were two partners, the bosses. And one guy who owned-- Maury Leshner-- I remember the names. Maury Leshner came to me and he said, you know, I have the Big Town Delicatessen Market? You know, in Big Town Market, the deli department? Why don't you come and work for me? It's a union job, you work 40 hours, and if you want to work Sunday, double. How much is an hour? $3.25. All right, Sunday, double. I go, of course I want to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I went over there to Leo, which was my boss at the time, Leo. I'm telling you the truth. This is it. I want to give you two weeks notice. I just don't want to leave you alone. He said, you know what, just go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was a person who was willing to give him notice. You know what I mean? Why? I told him, well it's a union job. They have benefits. They have Kaiser, you know, I mean, everything. You don't have anything. He says, well just-- you now what? Here's your check, and don't come back tomorrow. OK. I went in next day to Maury Leshner. I told him, I can work, starting-- he says, stay here. Start now. And I worked there about for three months, when this Cy Jeffy who was the owner of the Three Stars Smoked Fish Company, come to me. He says, Fred, I don't want you to work for anybody anymore. I said, what do you want me to do? I want you to be your own boss, your own business. There's a store empty on Fairfax. Which, eventually, I stayed there over 40 years. Go find out-- for the refrigeration, find out how much it will cost you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him, Cy, all I have is $2,500. That's all we had. Don't worry. I'm a Swedish Jew. When I came to this country, that's how I got my break. Someone gave it to me, and now I'm giving it back to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I figured out it was going to cost about $12,000. You know what I mean? But you didn't need that much for a down payment, you know? Cy came. I told him $12,000. He says, how much money out of 20-- here is a $2,500 check. You have $5,000. I told him, where do I sign? You sign nowhere. I know you're going to pay it back if you make it, and if you don't make it, I will not sue you anyway. And that's how I started my business. I was there over 40 years, in the same location. Tell us what your business was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sold the business three years ago to a Russian Jew. I did not charge him a penny for the business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just said, anything in the store. The [INAUDIBLE], the equipment, everything. Only thing he paid me is from the-- for the inventory, the merchandise I had because I did not want to have any money either. I felt I'm doing-- giving back. And out of that, I'm doing fine, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the  name of the deli?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freddy's Deli. Same thing. They kept the name. They won't give it up, too many people knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me about your family now. Your children and what your life--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sons. They're both doctors. One is a pediatrician. One is an ear, nose, and throat surgeon and general intern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have five grandchildren. Four-- Four boys and one girl. Naturally, because of the fact that there is only one girl in the family, she's the favorite. Not that I don't love the other ones, you understand? It's no difference to me, basically. But you know, one girl in the family and you spoil them. What does your other son do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a pediatrician. The older son is a pediatrician. He has three boys. The younger son has a girl and a boy, and he's an ear, nose, and throat, like I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me ask you, now that it's almost half a century since-- since the end of the war, is there something you want to say to your children or to your grandchildren? Is there some kind of message you would like to pass on, or a lesson about what you've learned over all of these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really have a message for them. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Only thing I always ask them, to marry a Jewish girl, which they did, both of them. Not because I'm religious, because I don't want your grandfather turn in his grave. And they always honored that. Very good children, both of them. I cannot complain. This, I'm blessed and lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So many times as I was thinking in my life, I thought that you know what? I'm not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really doing a favor, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] by having children. You know what I mean? I really felt it. I never told my wife even.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I felt I'm really not doing them a favor because history repeats itself. We've seen so many times. But there's a giant of a difference. We have in Israel today. If there would have been Israel to Hitler, this would never would happen, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we live in the United States, right? And I cannot believe that any type of major disaster can happen to the Jews in the United States. I do not believe it. So message I give them? Thank God, you were born here and you are here. And I hope you never have to face. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Thank you. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the far right, it was me. Next to it is my father, then my brother. And next to my brother-- next to my mother is my brother, Charlie. There's my sister, Jolan. Underneath, on the left side, is my brother-in-law, Jolan's husband. And then it's Bundy and Tibi. This picture is the wedding and the whole-- the whole family. Sister's-- my sister's wedding, and all the children from both sides of the family there. This is a picture from my deli, which was named Freddy's Deli, which I'm retired from three years ago, me and my wife. This is a picture after the war, in 1946, after we got married, my wife and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of my three brothers and my sister. You see the cut out? That's me because after the war, when I came home and entered the university, I needed a picture. And there was no photographer, so I find this picture, I cut it out, and I gave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. All these pictures, I want to mention, we found it after we came home in 1945, in my parents' home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my mother, a beautiful lady. And all the other pictures from my children and grandchildren-- is on my wife tape, Ella.
